serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one buttermilk crispy chicken burger (approximately 250 g), comprising a deep-fried chicken thigh patty in a glazed bun with cheddar, lettuce, mayonnaise, pineapple, and purple cabbage.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken sandwich, crispy fried, on bun (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite); supplemented with HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient data for local burger composite
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input per_100g values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central crispy fried chicken sandwich composite (FDC ID 748967) and HPB Singapore Foodo burger category data. Values are consistent with the USDA composite for a deep-fried chicken thigh burger with bun, cheese, and condiments. Input values retained with medium confidence given alignment with USDA reference range. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not available in the input; set to -1. Potassium, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water, and several vitamins not reported in input; set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283; University of Sydney GI Database — hamburger bun / fried chicken sandwich composite analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for buttermilk crispy chicken burger. GI estimated at 66 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate vehicle being a refined glazed burger bun (GI ~70–75 per University of Sydney GI Database, white bread analogue) moderated by the protein and fat content of the fried chicken patty, cheese, and mayonnaise, which slow gastric emptying and reduce the effective GI of the composite meal. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (66 × 46.15 g carbohydrate in 250 g serving) / 100 = 30.4. This is consistent with published GL estimates for fried chicken sandwiches in the peer-reviewed literature.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 307.7 mg/100 g (769 mg per 250 g burger serving) combined with saturated fat 3.08 g/100 g and sugar 5.38 g/100 g from the glazed bun
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this burger due to its high sodium load (~769 mg per serving, representing ~33% of the 2300 mg daily limit), and should request a plain wholemeal bun, no added sauces, and avoid upsized set meals that include sugar-sweetened beverages.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (GL ~30 per serving) driven by refined glazed bun (GI ~66 composite), sugar 5.38 g/100 g, and low dietary fibre 1.23 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this burger due to its high glycemic load (~30 per serving), low fibre content, and refined carbohydrate base; if consumed, choose a wholemeal bun, remove the glaze, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.08 g/100 g (7.7 g per serving), trans fat 0.08 g/100 g (0.2 g per serving), cholesterol 27.69 mg/100 g, and deep-fried preparation generating pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this burger due to its saturated fat, trans fat, and deep-fried preparation; if consumed occasionally, request grilled chicken instead of deep-fried, omit the mayonnaise, and choose a plain bun to reduce saturated fat and trans fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs; trans fat 0.08 g/100 g from frying oil; refined sugars from glazed bun promote inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations such as this burger, as the AGEs generated during deep-frying and the trans fat content directly exacerbate joint inflammation; a grilled or baked chicken alternative is strongly preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Buttermilk Crispy Chicken Burger is a Western-style fast-food burger popular in Singapore, consisting of a whole-muscle chicken thigh marinated in buttermilk and deep-fried to a golden, crunchy exterior, served in a soft glazed burger bun. The burger is assembled with white cheddar cheese, crisp romaine lettuce, black pepper mayonnaise, grilled pineapple rings for a sweet-savoury contrast, and shredded purple cabbage for crunch and colour. The buttermilk marinade tenderises the chicken and contributes a mild tangy flavour, while the deep-frying process creates a crispy, well-seasoned crust. Per 100 g, the burger provides approximately 192 kcal, 8.5 g protein, 10 g fat (3.1 g saturated), 18.5 g carbohydrates, 5.4 g sugar, 1.2 g dietary fibre, and 308 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 66 (medium) due to the refined glazed bun, moderated by the fat and protein content of the filling. Clinically, this burger is high concern for hypertension (high sodium ~769 mg per serving), Type 2 Diabetes (high glycemic load ~30, low fibre), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, trans fat, deep-fried AGEs). It is halal-certified in the Singapore market context and commonly available on food delivery platforms. The dish is classified as ultra-processed due to the combination of deep-frying, glazed bun, and commercial condiments.

confidence_reason: Input per_100g values are ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from a GrabFood listing. Values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central crispy fried chicken sandwich composite data and HPB Singapore burger category ranges, supporting medium confidence. No exact approved-source match exists for this specific product; values are adapted from the closest USDA composite. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are not available from the input and are set to -1.
notes: 1. Per_100g values sourced from ai_generated GrabFood input (confidence 0.7), cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 748967 (crispy fried chicken sandwich composite) and HPB Singapore Foodo burger category. Values are within plausible range for this food type. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in the input data or confirmed from a single approved source for this specific composite dish. 3. potassium_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg set to -1: these values were not reported in the input and detailed micronutrient data for this specific Singapore fast-food composite are not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2026-04-16. 4. GI estimated at 66 (medium) using analogous food method: refined glazed burger bun GI ~70-75 (University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by fat and protein content of the composite. 5. The input health_suitability section referenced a soft drink and French fries as components of a set meal; the per_100g nutritional values and health context in this record apply to the burger only (250 g default portion). Set meal and upsized meal portions are preserved in serving notes but health context is assessed for the burger portion. 6. Three portions from input preserved: 1 burger (250 g, default), 1 set meal (550 g), 1 upsized meal (650 g). 7. trans_fat_g is 0.08 g/100 g (0.2 g per 250 g serving) — present at low but non-zero level from commercial deep-frying oil; this is a clinical concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis at any detectable level.
